game_name: Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis

danger_signals.visual_form: Character sprite with Nazi insignia or military uniform
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat; player should be cautious in dialogue and interactions

danger_signals.visual_form: Character drawing weapon during scripted sequence
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent danger; narrative checkpoint where player loses control temporarily

danger_signals.visual_form: Disheveled rooms, overturned furniture in Sophia's office
danger_signals.meaning: Recent hostile activity; antagonists have been here before player
Potential hazard zones; game designed to signal recognizable danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Character sprite in Team Path mode
safety_signals.meaning: Partnership available; support throughout game reduces isolation risk
Safe starting location; neutral ground before antagonist introduction

safety_signals.visual_form: Interior domestic space after initial escape
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary sanctuary; information gathering hub before adventure phase

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Nazi insignia visible on Klaus Kerner character
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Klaus Kerner disguised as 'Mr. Smith' (civilian appearance masking agent identity)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Military/scientific expedition equipment; ransacked spaces indicating aggressive search methodology

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Indiana Jones professor credentials at Barnett College
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Academic archives, college setting, archaeological equipment

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Orichalcum bead; mystical medallion with Atlantean iconography
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Mystical artifacts, necklace with beads, invocation of spirit of Nur-Ab-Sal

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Organized Barnett College archives; students/faculty in normal activity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs engaged in routine tasks; no combat or evasion mechanics active

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Klaus Kerner's disguised entry with weapon drawn; sudden shift to danger
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Indy escapes; rapid travel to warn Sophia; narrative shifts to pursuit mode

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Orichalcum bead inserted into medallion; visual effect of spirit invocation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Exposition sequence with Sophia explaining Nur-Ab-Sal and Dr. Ubermann's goals

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple international locations including Algiers souk; diverse architectural styles
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player explores static environments, interacts with varied NPCs, constructs verb-based commands

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Team Path shows Sophia as visible companion sprite; Fists Path emphasizes combat sprites; Wits Path highlights puzzle-solving UI elements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Different cutscenes, NPC interaction sets, and environmental puzzle types based on chosen path

environmental_storytelling.example: Klaus Kerner's dropped coat reveals Nazi identity card and magazine article about Indy's past with Sophia—single object conveys faction, history, and plot connection
Sophia's ransacked office demonstrates antagonist's prior activity and desperation; broken/disheveled state communicates hostile intent without dialogue

environmental_storytelling.technique: Artifact visual design as narrative anchor
environmental_storytelling.example: Orichalcum bead's 'sparkling metal' appearance and mystical properties (invoking Atlantean king) establish supernatural stakes visually before exposition
Academic Barnett College (ordered, institutional) vs. dangerous global locales (Algiers souk) vs. mystical ritual spaces—visual environment signals faction and tone
Klaus Kerner presented as 'Mr. Smith' civilian visitor—visual disguise creates dramatic irony and teaches player to distrust surface appearances

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Polite visitor requesting artifact; uses key to open statue; pulls weapon when advantage gained; escapes with stolen items
Pursues Indy and Sophia; searches for additional Atlantean artifacts; acts as primary threat throughout early narrative
Lectures on Atlantis; explains Nazi threat; provides companion support; reveals personal artifact possession
Performs mystical invocation with orichalcum bead; channels Atlantean king spirit; connects archaeology to supernatural mechanics
Retrieves statue on request; escapes Klaus; travels to warn Sophia; shifts from passive curator to active adventurer
Implied through exposition as orchestrating Nazi search for Atlantean power; creates highest-level antagonist motivation without early direct appearance

notes: Fate of Atlantis uses classic SCUMM point-and-click visual language: sprite-based characters, static backgrounds, verb-based interaction UI (Pick up, Use, Talk to). Danger is communicated through narrative cutscenes (Klaus's gun) rather than real-time combat telegraphing—game explicitly designed dangerous situations to be 'easily recognizable' to avoid unfair deaths. The three-path system (Team/Wits/Fists) creates visual behavioral shifts: companion sprite appears/disappears, puzzle-heavy UI elements populate, combat animations become prominent. Faction identity relies heavily on costume (Nazi insignia), artifact design (mystical beads), and location aesthetics (institutional vs. global exploration). World-state transitions marked by cutscene visual effects (spirit invocation glow) and environmental changes (ransacking, location shifts). Environmental storytelling privileges object details (identity cards, magazine articles, necklaces) over systemic mechanics.
